messages.content: improve：Interestingly, compared with fibrosis, the proportion of NK cells in the blood of fibrosis mice receiving IL-10 intervention decreased, and the proportion of peripheral NK cells (CNK) characterized by (cd49a-cd49b+) in the liver increased, while the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) characterized by (cd49a+cd49b-) decreased

Interestingly, it was observed that the administration of IL-10 intervention to fibrosis mice resulted in a decrease in the proportion of NK cells in their blood, as opposed to fibrosis alone. Additionally, there was an increase in the proportion of peripheral NK cells (CNK) characterized by (cd49a-cd49b+) in the liver, while the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) characterized by (cd49a+cd49b-) decreased.

improve：Notably, there was a decrease in the proportion of NK cells in the bloodstream in fibrotic mice following IL-10 intervention. 
Specifically, Further analysis of liver NK cell typing revealed that there was a significant increase in the proportion of peripheral NK cells (CNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+) and a decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-).

Intriguingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice led to a notable decrease in the proportion of NK cells in the bloodstream. However, upon further examination of liver NK cell subpopulations, it was found that there was a significant increase in the proportion of peripheral NK cells (CNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+). Conversely, there was a decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-).
improve:After IL-10 treatment in fibrotic mice, the number of NK cells in the liver with strong cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion ability significantly increased

Remarkably, following IL-10 treatment in fibrotic mice, there was a substantial increase in the population of NK cells within the liver that displayed enhanced cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities.

IMPROVE:Previous studies have demonstrated that intervention with IL-10 effectively enhances the quantity and functionality of NK cells in liver tissue. The level of NK cell maturity is closely linked to their functionality, with three distinct developmental stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+). Following IL-10 treatment in fibrotic mice, there was a substantial increase in the population of developing NK cells within the liver that displayed enhanced cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities.
Intriguingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice led to a notable decrease in the proportion of NK cells in the bloodstream. Upon further examination of liver NK cell subpopulations, it was it was found that the proportion of peripheral NK cells (CNK) characterized by (cd49a-cd49b+) significantly increased, while the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) characterized by (cd49a+cd49b-) decreased.

Previous studies have provided compelling evidence indicating that IL-10 intervention effectively enhances both the quantity and functionality of NK cells within liver tissue. It is worth noting that the functionality of NK cells is closely associated with their maturity levels, which can be classified into three distinct stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+). In the context of fibrotic mice receiving IL-10 treatment, a significant increase was observed in the population of developing NK cells within the liver. These developing NK cells exhibited remarkable cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities, indicating the beneficial effects of IL-10 intervention on NK cell functionality.

Intriguingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice resulted in a notable reduction in the proportion of NK cells within the bloodstream. Further analysis of liver NK cell subpopulations revealed that the proportion of peripheral NK cells (CNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+) exhibited a substantial increase. Conversely, there was a significant decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-). These findings shed light on the specific impact of IL-10 intervention on different NK cell populations in both the systemic and hepatic environments.
improve

Significantly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice yielded noteworthy results on the NK cell population. Firstly, previous studies have demonstrated that IL-10 treatment effectively enhances both the quantity and functionality of NK cells in liver tissue. NK cell maturity is strongly correlated with their functionality, and they can be categorized into three distinct stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+).

In the context of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice, a remarkable increase in the population of developing NK cells within the liver was observed. These developing NK cells exhibited enhanced capabilities in terms of cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion, indicating the positive impact of IL-10 on NK cell functionality.

Furthermore, it is worth noting that there was an interesting shift in the distribution of NK cells following IL-10 treatment. Notably, the proportion of NK cells in the bloodstream decreased significantly. However, a more detailed analysis of liver NK cell subpopulations unveiled intriguing changes. Specifically, there was a significant increase in the proportion of peripheral NK cells (CNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+). In contrast, there was a notable decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-). These findings shed light on the specific effects of IL-10 intervention on different subsets of NK cells, both systemically and within the liver.

In conclusion, IL-10 treatment in fibrotic mice led to a remarkable increase in the population of developing NK cells in the liver, which exhibited enhanced cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities. Additionally, there were changes in the distribution of NK cells, with a decrease in the proportion of NK cells in the bloodstream and an increase in peripheral NK cells in the liver. This highlights the differential impact of IL-10 intervention on distinct NK cell subsets.

improve：Previous studies have provided compelling evidence indicating that IL-10 intervention effectively enhances both the quantity and functionality of NK cells within liver tissue. It is worth noting that the functionality of NK cells is closely associated with their maturity levels, which can be classified into three distinct stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+). In the context of fibrotic mice receiving IL-10 treatment, a significant increase was observed in the population of developing NK cells within the liver. These developing NK cells exhibited remarkable cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities, indicating the beneficial effects of IL-10 intervention on NK cell functionality.
Intriguingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice resulted in a notable reduction in the proportion of NK cells within the bloodstream. Further analysis of liver NK cell subpopulations revealed that the proportion of peripheral classic NK cells (cNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+) exhibited a substantial increase. Conversely, there was a significant decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-). Additionally, among various chemokines present in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its distinct ability to attract NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group, highlighting its potential role in the observed increase of NK cell presence in the liver following IL-10 intervention.These findings suggest a potential role for IL-10 in modulating NK cell distribution and functionality, ultimately contributing to the regulation of the immune response in the liver microenvironment during fibrosis.

Previous studies have provided compelling evidence indicating that IL-10 intervention effectively enhances both the quantity and functionality of NK cells within liver tissue. It is worth noting that the functionality of NK cells is closely associated with their maturity levels, which can be classified into three distinct stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+). Within the context of fibrotic mice receiving IL-10 treatment, a significant increase was observed in the population of developing NK cells within the liver. These developing NK cells exhibited remarkable cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities, further highlighting the beneficial effects of IL-10 intervention on NK cell functionality.

Intriguingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice was also found to result in a notable reduction in the proportion of NK cells within the bloodstream. To gain further insights into this observation, a detailed analysis of liver NK cell subpopulations was conducted. This analysis revealed a significant increase in the proportion of peripheral classic NK cells (cNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+). Conversely, there was a significant decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-). These findings indicate that IL-10 intervention has the potential to modulate the distribution and balance of distinct NK cell subsets in the liver microenvironment.

Moreover, among various chemokines present in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its distinct ability to attract NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group. This finding suggests a potential mechanism underlying the observed increase in NK cell presence in the liver following IL-10 intervention. The upregulation of CCL5 may facilitate the recruitment of NK cells to the liver microenvironment, contributing to the dynamic changes observed in NK cell distribution and functionality upon IL-10 treatment.

Taken together, these findings provide important insights into the role of IL-10 in modulating NK cell distribution and functionality, ultimately contributing to the regulation of the immune response within the liver microenvironment during fibrosis. Further investigation is warranted to fully elucidate the underlying mechanisms and potential therapeutic applications of IL-10 in the context of liver fibrosis.

It is noteworthy that the functionality of NK cells is closely associated with their maturity levels, which can be classified into three distinct stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+). In the context of fibrotic mice receiving IL-10 treatment, a significant increase was observed in the population of developing NK cells within the liver. These developing NK cells exhibited remarkable cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities, indicating the beneficial effects of IL-10 intervention on NK cell functionality. Interestingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice resulted in a notable reduction in the proportion of NK cells circulating in the bloodstream. Further analysis of liver NK cell subpopulations revealed that the proportion of peripheral classic NK cells (cNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+) exhibited a substantial increase. Conversely, there was a significant decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-). Moreover, among several chemokines detected in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its specific ability to attract NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group, emphasizing its potential role in the observed increase in NK cell presence in the liver following IL-10 intervention. These findings collectively suggest a crucial role for IL-10 in modulating both the distribution and functionality of NK cells, ultimately contributing to the regulation of the immune response within the liver microenvironment during fibrosis. Further investigation is warranted to fully elucidate the underlying mechanisms and potential therapeutic applications of IL-10 in the context of liver fibrosis.
Title: IL-10 Intervention Enhances NK Cell Quantity and Functionality in Liver Tissue during Fibrosis

Summarize this paragraph in short sentences：Previous studies have provided compelling evidence indicating that IL-10 intervention effectively enhances both the quantity and functionality of NK cells within the liver tissue. It is noteworthy that the functionality of NK cells is closely associated with their maturity levels, which can be classified into three distinct stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+). In the context of fibrotic mice receiving IL-10 treatment, a significant increase was observed in the population of developing NK cells within the liver. These developing NK cells exhibited remarkable cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities, indicating the beneficial effects of IL-10 intervention on NK cell functionality. Interestingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice resulted in a notable reduction in the proportion of NK cells circulating in the bloodstream. Further analysis of liver NK cell subpopulations revealed that the proportion of peripheral classic NK cells (cNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+) exhibited a substantial increase. Conversely, there was a significant decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-). Moreover, among several chemokines detected in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its specific ability to attract NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group, emphasizing its potential role in the observed increase in NK cell presence in the liver following IL-10 intervention. These findings collectively suggest a crucial role for IL-10 in modulating both the distribution and functionality of NK cells, ultimately contributing to the regulation of the immune response within the liver microenvironment during fibrosis. Further investigation is warranted to fully elucidate the underlying mechanisms and potential therapeutic applications of IL-10 in the context of liver fibrosis.

IL-10 intervention enhances the quantity and functionality of NK cells in the liver during fibrosis. Increasing numbers of developing NK cells with enhanced cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion were observed. Interestingly, IL-10 decreased NK cell proportion in the bloodstream, while increasing peripheral classic NK cells in the liver. CCL5, a chemokine attracting NK cells, showed a significant increase. IL-10 modulates NK cell distribution and functionality, influencing the immune response in liver fibrosis. Further research is needed for better understanding and potential therapeutic uses.

improve：Moreover, among several chemokines detected in the liver, including chemokine C-C motif ligand 2, C-C motif ligand 3 and C-C motif ligand 5 (CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5), it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its specific ability to promote the development and migration of NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group, emphasizing its potential role in the observed increase in NK cell accumulation in the liver following IL-10 gene intervention.

Furthermore, among the several chemokines detected in the liver, including C-C motif ligand 2, C-C motif ligand 3, and C-C motif ligand 5 (CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5), it was observed that only CCL5, which is known for its specific ability to enhance the development and migration of NK cells, exhibited a significant increase in the IL-10 group. This finding highlights the potential role of CCL5 in facilitating the accumulation of NK cells in the liver after IL-10 gene intervention.

improve：Moreover, among several chemokines detected in the liver, including chemokine C-C motif ligand 2, C-C motif ligand 3 and C-C motif ligand 5 (CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5), it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its specific ability to promote the development and migration of NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group, emphasizing its potential role in the observed increase in NK cell accumulation in the liver following IL-10 gene intervention.

Furthermore, among the various chemokines detected in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it is noteworthy that only CCL5, renowned for its capability to facilitate the development and migration of NK cells, exhibited a substantial increase in the IL-10 group. This observation further underscores the potential involvement of CCL5 in the observed augmentation of NK cell accumulation in the liver following IL-10 gene intervention.

improve：Figure 3. NK cell depletion antagonized the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10. ASGM1 was used to deplete NK cells in IL-10-treated fibrotic mice. IL-10 treatment combined with NK cell depletion group (IL-10/ASGM1) were intraperitoneally injected with 20μl of ASGM1 solution per mouse, twice a week starting from the third week, while the remaining mice were injected with rabbit IgG as an isotype control. A verifies the effect of ASGM1 in depleting NK cells in mice liver. B shows histopathological damage in mouse liver. C shows collagen deposition in mouse liver. D shows area analysis of positive collagen fibers in liver stained with Sirius-Red. E shows ALT and AST contents in mice serum. F shows HYP contents in mice liver. Groups: Fibrosis control group (FIBROSIS), IL-10 treatment group (IL-10), IL-10 treatment combined with NK cell depletion group (IL-10/ASGM1). Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 5-10, *p <0.05, **p <0.01, ***p <0.001.

Title: NK Cell Depletion Suppresses the Anti-Fibrotic Effect of IL-10 Treatment

Figure 3 depicts the impact of NK cell depletion on the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10. NK cells were depleted using ASGM1 in IL-10-treated fibrotic mice. The IL-10/ASGM1 group received intraperitoneal injections of 20μl ASGM1 solution per mouse, twice a week starting from the third week, while the remaining mice received rabbit IgG as an isotype control. 

Panel A demonstrates the efficacy of ASGM1 in depleting NK cells in the liver of mice. Panel B showcases histopathological damage in the mouse liver, while Panel C displays collagen
